calendar.matters:
ASBESTOS ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Nov-06-2025 Dismissal

ASBESTOS ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Sep-29-2025 Continued To Nov-06-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 304

MASTER CALENDAR JURY ASBESTOS
Off Calendar Feb-06-2025 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On Oct-02-25 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 304

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION As To Plaintiffs Fraud Cause Of Action
Off Calendar Jan-08-2025 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jan-21-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
(PART 1 OF 3)
No appearances.

RULING:
Not contested and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Longs Drug Stores California LLC's Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed. No Reply filed.

Plaintiffs' complaint alleges that Ivan James You's ("Decedent") mesothelioma was a result of his family's use of Johnson's Baby Power ("Baby Powder") purchased at Defendant Longs Drug Stores California LLC's ("Defendant") stores from 1988 to 1992. Defendant moves for summary judgment, or in the alternative, summary adjudication. Defendant argues Plaintiffs have no evidence that Decedent obtained an asbestos-containing product from Defendant's stores or that any such alleged exposure was a substantial factor in causing his mesothelioma. Defendant also seeks summary adjudication as to the causes of action for: Negligence I-Survival, Products Liability-Survival, Negligence I-Wrongful Death, Products Liability-Wrongful Death, and Defendant's failure to inspect and test talcum power products.

"[S]ummary judgment law in this state [no] longer require[s] a defendant moving for summary judgment to conclusively negate an element of the plaintiff's cause of action." (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal. 4th 826, 853.) "The defendant may also present evidence that the plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence." (Id. at 855.) A defendant may "base its summary judgment motion on plaintiffs' minimal discovery responses, contending both that its discovery, in the form of [a plaintiff's] deposition and special interrogatories, was comprehensive and that plaintiffs' factually devoid responses indicated that they could not prove causation." (Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal. App. 4th 96, 103.) In asbestos cases, a defendant's initial burden is met when "plaintiff's deposition testimony made clear that he had no knowledge himself that he was exposed to one of [defendant]'s asbestos-containing products" and interrogatory responses that "does not state specific facts showing that [plaintiff] was actually exposed to asbestos-containing material from [defendant]'s products." (Id. at 104.)

Defendant argues Plaintiffs have no evidence that (1) Plaintiffs purchased the Baby Powder from Defendant's stores; (2) the Baby Powder used on Decedent or in Decedent's presence actually contained asbestos; and (3) the exposure from the Baby Powder obtained from Defendant was a substantial factor in causing Decedent's mesothelioma. (Motion at pp. 9-10.) However, Defendant's contention that Plaintiff's discovery responses are factually devoid (id. at 11-13) is demonstrably false. Defendant disregards, among other things, Plaintiff's extensive written interrogatory responses. (Defense Exhibit N.) Defendant also failed to depose Johnson & Johnson, the manufacturer of the Baby Powder, which Plaintiff identified as a key witness with knowledge of the asbestos content of the Baby Powder. (Exhibit N at 6.) Defendant therefore failed to shift the burden to Plaintiff, and its motion for summary judgment must be denied on that ground alone.
(Mini Minutes continued, see next entry)

LONGS DRUG STORES CALIFORNIA, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
Off Calendar Jan-21-2025 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: (PART 2 OF 3)
(Mini Minutes continued from previous entry)

First, Defendant contends that Plaintiffs have no evidence that Decedent was exposed to asbestos from the Baby Powder purchased from Defendant, citing Plaintiff Collen You's, the mother of Decedent, deposition testimony. The Court disagrees. As Plaintiffs noted in their Opposition, Ms. You testified the Baby Powder she used on and around Decedent was from Defendant stores. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit A at 83:8-22.) When asked if she purchased Baby Powder from any other locations, Ms. You testified, "basically, it was Longs Drugs then." (Id.) She even testified that she purchased Baby Powder from Longs from two possible store locations (1) El Camino Road in San Carlos, California; and (2) 42nd Street in San Mateo, California.

(Id.) The fact that Ms. You did not recall specific instances when she purchased the Baby Powder from Defendant's stores or that she did not keep the containers of Baby Powder she used, does not create an inference of non-exposure or the inability to prove exposure through other sources of evidence than Plaintiff's testimony. To the contrary, even if the moving defendant carries its initial burden, "it is not uncommon for asbestos plaintiffs to rely on their own testimony or testimony by family members or coworkers, i.e., from individuals other than experts, or on business records detailing the purchase and placement of asbestos products at jobsites or in work environments. Even circumstantial evidence ... sufficient to support a reasonable inference of exposure may defeat summary judgment." (LAOSD Asbestos Cases (2020) 44 Cal.App.5th 475, 488-489 (cleaned up).) In any event, a plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting her claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439 (emphasis added).)

Second, Defendant argues Plaintiffs' discovery responses are factually devoid because they do not contain evidence that the Baby Powder Plaintiffs purchased from Defendant's stores contained asbestos. The Court disagrees. Ms. You's "ALL FACT" special interrogatory responses state, for example, that Ms. You bought Baby Powder from Defendant's store and used it on Decedent when he was a baby between 1988 and 1992. (Defense Exhibit N at 2:15-3:11.) That all Johnson & Johnson Baby Powder purchased from Defendant was manufactured with asbestos-containing talc and its inhalation was a substantial factor in causing Decedent's disease. (Id. at 2:15-4:9.) To support that claim, Plaintiffs identified exhibits that have been used in multiple trials against Johnson & Johnson to show its Baby Powder was manufactured with asbestos-containing talc, including advertisements, scientific studies, test results, pictures, samples, testimony, letters, mine studies, lab results, reports, and discovery responses. (Id. at 8:1-28:16.) The response then stated that Johnson & Johnson had knowledge of asbestos in its Baby Powder. (Id. at 38:17-45:28.) The discovery response also referred to Defendant's corporate representatives and Johnson & Johnson corporate representatives as persons with knowledge of the facts. (Id. at 6:25-7:13.) Plaintiffs identified numerous documents, including testimony, scientific studies, and reports showing that exposure to the asbestos-containing Baby Powder caused Decedent's disease. (Id. at 48:1-53:11.) Defendant failed to address the response and did not provide any contrary evidence. Defendant did not submit any expert testimony establishing that the Baby Powder was asbestos free or contained talc from asbestos-free mines. Nor did Defendant submit any other evidence establishing that Plaintiffs cannot obtain evidence of asbestos in the Baby Powder.
(Mini Minutes continued, see next entry)

LONGS DRUG STORES CALIFORNIA, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
Off Calendar Jan-21-2025 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: (PART 3 OF 3)
(Mini Minutes continued from previous entry)

Lastly, Defendant argues that Plaintiffs have no evidence that the exposure from the Baby Powder obtained from Defendant was a substantial factor in causing Decedent's mesothelioma. Even if Defendant had met its initial burden on this motion, which it has not, Plaintiffs' evidence establishes the existence of a disputed issue of fact. Plaintiffs filed an expert declaration from Dr. Richard Cohen stating that Ms. You's use of the asbestos-containing Baby Powder in Decedent's presence was a substantial factor in causing his mesothelioma and death. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit B at 79-80, 1-84.) Thus, Defendant fails to foreclose the possibility that Plaintiffs may yet prove asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant.

(See, e.g., Strobel v. Johnson & Johnson (2021) 70 Cal.App.5th 796, 802-803, 811-828 [reversing summary judgment where plaintiff presented conflicting expert declarations, including declaration of Dr. Cohen, which confirmed the presence of asbestos in the talcum ore Johnson & Johnson used to manufacture Johnson's Baby Powder]; Lyon v. Colgate-Palmolive Co. (2017) 16 Cal.App.5th 463, 471 [reversing summary judgment for defendant where plaintiff presented expert declaration confirming the presence of asbestos in mine sources and defendant's talcum powder product, which created triable issue].)

Defendants Motion for Summary Adjudication is DENIED.

Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication as to the First through Fourth Causes of Action is DENIED for the reasons discussed above.

Defendant's motion for Summary Adjudication as to its alleged duty to test or inspect the products it sold is DENIED. The Court notes that Defendant identified the wrong party in its separate statement (i.e., Safeway). Regardless, the Court reviewed the arguments presented by both parties concerning Defendant's duty to inspect or test a product it sold. Defendant contends that its role does not include the duty to inspect or test products it sold, relying principally on Tourte v. Horton Manufacturing Company (1930) 108 Cal. App. 22. (Opening Brief, 22-23.) However, the continued viability of that dated authority is dubious. As Defendant concedes, Tourte was decided decades ago, long before the development of modern strict liability law. (See, e.g., Jiminez v. Superior Court (2002) 29 Cal.4th 473, 477 [noting that, a year after "California became the first state to allow recovery for strict products liability," Vandermark v. Ford Motor Co. (1964) 61 Cal.2d 256 "extended strict products liability to retailers" (cleaned up)].) "The theories of negligence and strict liability parallel and supplement each other, and the same policy considerations that militate for or against imposition of strict liability may apply with equal force in the context of negligence." (Bettencourt v. Hennessy Industries, Inc. (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 1103, 1118 (cleaned up).) While Defendant argues that the rule that a seller of products has no duty to inspect products manufactured by others for latent defects remains good law for purposes of negligence liability, that is insufficient to warrant summary adjudication. Even if that remains the law, Defendant presents no evidence that it did not know and had no reason to believe that the Baby Powder was defective. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: A. Mok. Not reported. (304/EPS)

LONGS DRUG STORES CALIFORNIA, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
Off Calendar Jan-21-2025 Off Calendar
